sections.title: рҹ§’ The Story вҖ” Why Cloud Was Invented
sections.rawMarkdown:
Imagine it's 2004. You want to build a website like Facebook.

**What you had to do before cloud:**

```
Month 1-3  вҶ’ Order servers ($5,000вҖ“$20,000 each). Wait for delivery.
Month 4    вҶ’ Rent space in a data center. Pay for power + cooling + internet.
Month 5-6  вҶ’ Set up OS, networking, firewalls, DNS, load balancers.
Month 6+   вҶ’ Finally write your app. But how many servers did you buy?
             Too few  вҶ’ site crashes when popular. Revenue lost.
             Too many вҶ’ paying for idle machines. Money wasted.
Forever    вҶ’ Your engineers maintain hardware instead of building features.

Total cost before writing ONE line of code: $200,000+
Time before launch: 6+ months
```

**Amazon had this exact problem internally.** Every team waited months for servers. In ~2003, they built an internal platform: any team could get a server in minutes, pay per hour, return when done.

In 2006 they realized: *every company has this problem.* They opened it to the public вҶ’ **AWS was born.**

- **March 2006** вҶ’ S3 launched ($0.15/GB/month)  
- **August 2006** вҶ’ EC2 launched (beta)  
- **2010** вҶ’ Amazon.com itself migrated to AWS  
- **2023** вҶ’ AWS revenue: $91 billion/year, 31% of global cloud market

---

sections.title: рҹ“җ What Cloud Computing Actually Is
sections.rawMarkdown:
> **Simple version:** Renting computing power over the internet, paying only for what you use, scaling up or down in seconds.

### Cloud Big Picture Diagram
```
Your team needs infrastructure
           в”Ӯ
           в–ј
      AWS service catalog
           в”Ӯ
   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
   в–ј       в–ј        в–ј        в–ј        в–ј
  EC2      S3      RDS      VPC    Lambda
   в”Ӯ
   в–ј
Provision in minutes, scale on demand, pay only for usage

MENTAL MODEL:
Cloud is a menu of rentable building blocks, not one big server.
```

**Official NIST definition (broken down):**

| Term | Plain English |
|------|--------------|
| On-demand | Get it when you need it. No waiting, no approval. |
| Network access | Access over the internet from anywhere |
| Shared pool | AWS's millions of servers, you borrow some |
| Rapidly provisioned | Server in 90 seconds. Delete in 30 seconds. |
| Minimal effort | You don't manage the building, power, or hardware |

---

sections.title: 5пёҸвғЈ The 5 Characteristics of Cloud (NIST вҖ” Must Know)
sections.rawMarkdown:
> If a service is missing even one of these вҶ’ it is NOT truly cloud. Use this to challenge any vendor's "cloud" claim.

### 1. On-Demand Self-Service
```
You вҶ’ AWS Console вҶ’ Click "Launch" вҶ’ Server ready in 90 seconds
No phone call. No ticket. No waiting for human approval. 24/7/365.

VIOLATION EXAMPLE: "Managed hosting where you email support to add a server"
вҶ’ On-demand? Yes. Self-service? NO. Not real cloud.
```

### 2. Broad Network Access
```
Access your AWS infrastructure from:
вҶ’ Laptop in India, phone in USA, tablet in Brazil вҶ’ all same console
вҶ’ Standard protocols: HTTPS, SSH, RDP вҖ” no special software
вҶ’ Your app in AWS reachable from anywhere on Earth instantly

IMPORTANT: "Broad network access" вү  your data is public.
It means ACCESSIBLE VIA network, not EXPOSED TO the internet.
```

### 3. Resource Pooling (Multi-tenancy)
```
ONE physical AWS server вҶ’ runs HUNDREDS of virtual machines
Your EC2 might share hardware with Netflix's EC2.
But they are COMPLETELY ISOLATED. You cannot see each other.

How isolation works:
вҶ’ Hypervisor (Nitro) creates virtual boundaries in hardware
вҶ’ Your VM: dedicated virtual CPU, RAM, disk, network
вҶ’ If Netflix's app crashes вҶ’ yours is fine. Always.

Why this enables low cost:
вҶ’ AWS buys millions of servers
вҶ’ Costs divided across millions of customers
вҶ’ Your share: tiny fraction of actual hardware cost
вҶ’ This is why $0.01/hour for a server is possible
```

### 4. Rapid Elasticity
```
Normal day:    2 servers  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
Black Friday:  2 вҶ’ 50 servers in 3 minutes  в–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳв–Ҳ
After sale:    50 вҶ’ 2 servers. Stop paying.  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

"Appears unlimited" вҶ’ AWS has finite resources but so massive,
you can request thousands of servers in seconds.

VIOLATION EXAMPLE: "Shared hosting where you fill a form to upgrade plan"
вҶ’ Not rapid. Takes hours/days. Not elastic.

OLD WORLD PROBLEM eliminated:
"How many servers will we need next year?" вҶ’ Nobody knows.
Buy too few вҶ’ crashes. Buy too many вҶ’ wasteful.
AWS вҶ’ start with 2, auto-scale to 500, back to 2. Pay exactly.
```

### 5. Measured Service (Pay-as-you-go)
```
EVERYTHING is metered:
EC2      вҶ’ per second (min 60 seconds)
Lambda   вҶ’ per millisecond
S3       вҶ’ per GB stored/month + per 1000 API calls
RDS      вҶ’ per hour + per GB storage
Transfer вҶ’ per GB out to internet

Like electricity: you pay for exact units consumed.
Use nothing вҶ’ pay $0. Use a lot вҶ’ pay for exactly that.

GOTCHA: Some pricing is complex. Set billing alerts IMMEDIATELY
        after creating an AWS account. First bill shocks beginners.
```

---

sections.title: рҹҸ—пёҸ Service Models вҖ” IaaS, PaaS, SaaS
sections.rawMarkdown:
> **Pizza analogy:** Made at home = you do everything. Restaurant dine-in = they cook, you eat there. Delivery = they do everything.

```
                        YOU MANAGE вҶҗвҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҖ”вҶ’ AWS MANAGES
                        
On-Premise    IaaS          PaaS              SaaS
(your DC)    (EC2)    (Elastic Beanstalk)   (Gmail)
    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ
App code       YOU            YOU             THEM
OS patches     YOU           THEM             THEM
Runtime        YOU           THEM             THEM
Scaling        YOU           THEM             THEM
Hardware      THEM           THEM             THEM
Data center   THEM           THEM             THEM
```

### IaaS вҖ” Infrastructure as a Service
```
You get    вҶ’ raw virtual server (CPU, RAM, disk, network)
You manage вҶ’ everything from OS upward
AWS manages вҶ’ physical hardware, hypervisor, data center

AWS examples: EC2, VPC, EBS, EFS
Best for: teams needing full control, custom OS config, lift-and-shift migrations

ANALOGY: Renting a plot of land with foundation.
You build the house, furnish it, fix it when it breaks.
```

### PaaS вҖ” Platform as a Service  
```
You get    вҶ’ a platform to deploy your code
You manage вҶ’ application code + data only
AWS manages вҶ’ OS patches, runtime, scaling, servers, networking

AWS examples: Elastic Beanstalk, RDS, Lambda (partially)
Best for: small teams, focus on code not infrastructure, rapid prototyping

ANALOGY: Renting a fully equipped kitchen.
Oven, pots, fridge all there. You just cook.
```

### SaaS вҖ” Software as a Service
```
You get    вҶ’ complete working software over the internet
You manage вҶ’ your data and user settings
AWS manages вҶ’ everything (app, OS, hardware, networking, scaling)

Examples: Gmail, Salesforce, Zoom, Slack, GitHub
AWS SaaS products: WorkMail, Amazon Chime, Connect
Best for: end users needing a tool, not developers building one

ANALOGY: Going to a restaurant. You just eat.
```

---

sections.title: рҹҢҚ Deployment Models
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Public Cloud
```
Infrastructure: owned + operated by AWS
Tenancy:        shared (multi-tenant, isolated)
Cost:           pay-as-you-go, no CAPEX
Scale:          unlimited (practically)

Best for: startups, variable workloads, global reach
Examples: Netflix, Airbnb, Slack, Zoom вҶ’ all on AWS

Limitation: data sovereignty concerns for some regulated industries
```

### Private Cloud
```
Infrastructure: company owns and operates their own data center
Tenancy:        single-tenant (dedicated to one org)
Cost:           high upfront CAPEX, ongoing OPEX
Scale:          limited by hardware you own

Best for: classified government, highly regulated banking, defense
Tools: VMware vSphere, OpenStack, Microsoft Hyper-V
Problem: still has capacity planning issues. Just YOUR servers.
```

### Hybrid Cloud
```
Mix: on-premise (or private cloud) + public cloud, connected securely

COMMON PATTERNS:
1. Bursting вҶ’ normal on-premise, spike to AWS during peak
2. Data separation вҶ’ sensitive data on-prem, app layer on AWS
3. Migration phase вҶ’ gradually moving services to AWS

AWS tools for hybrid:
вҶ’ AWS Direct Connect: dedicated fiber from your office to AWS
вҶ’ AWS VPN: encrypted tunnel from your network to AWS VPC  
вҶ’ AWS Outposts: AWS hardware IN your data center (AWS-managed)
вҶ’ Storage Gateway: sync on-premise storage with S3

REAL EXAMPLE:
Large hospital:
вҶ’ Patient records: on-premise (HIPAA, never leaves building)
вҶ’ Hospital website: AWS (public, scalable)
вҶ’ Medical imaging processing: AWS (compute-intensive bursts)
вҶ’ Connection: Direct Connect (private fiber, not internet)
```

### Multi-Cloud
```
Using AWS + GCP + Azure simultaneously

WHY companies say they do it:
вҶ’ Avoid vendor lock-in
вҶ’ Best service from each (AWS for EC2, GCP for ML, Azure for Office)
вҶ’ Some regulatory requirements

REALITY CHECK:
вҶ’ 80% of workloads on one provider anyway
вҶ’ True multi-cloud = 2x the complexity, 2x the skills needed
вҶ’ Data transfer BETWEEN clouds = expensive
вҶ’ Most "multi-cloud" companies just have different teams on different clouds

Tools that help: Kubernetes (same containers anywhere), Terraform (IaC across clouds)
```

---

sections.title: рҹ’° Pricing Deep Dive
sections.rawMarkdown:
### The 3 Fundamentals

```
1. COMPUTE вҖ” pay for what you RUN
   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
   в”Ӯ EC2       вҶ’ per second (min 60s)                        в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ Lambda    вҶ’ per millisecond of execution                в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ Fargate   вҶ’ per vCPU-hour + per GB-memory-hour         в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ Stopped EC2 вҶ’ $0 compute (still pay EBS volume!)       в”Ӯ
   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

2. STORAGE вҖ” pay for what you STORE
   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
   в”Ӯ S3 Standard  вҶ’ $0.023 /GB/month                        в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ EBS gp3      вҶ’ $0.08  /GB/month (even if empty!)      в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ RDS          вҶ’ per hour (instance) + per GB (storage)  в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ DynamoDB     вҶ’ per GB stored + per million requests    в”Ӯ
   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

3. DATA TRANSFER вҖ” pay for what moves OUT
   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
   в”Ӯ IN to AWS           вҶ’ FREE (always)                    в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ Within same AZ      вҶ’ FREE                             в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ Between AZs         вҶ’ ~$0.01/GB each direction         в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ Between regions     вҶ’ $0.02вҖ“$0.09/GB                  в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ OUT to internet     вҶ’ $0.09/GB (first 10TB/month)     в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ OUT via CloudFront  вҶ’ $0.0085/GB (much cheaper!)      в”Ӯ
   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

> вҡ пёҸ **Hidden cost trap:** Serving 100TB/month directly from EC2 = **$9,000 just in transfer fees.** Use CloudFront вҖ” same content, 90% cheaper transfer cost.

### Real Cost Example вҖ” Lambda
```
Function: 128MB memory, 200ms execution, 10 million calls/month

Requests: (10M - 1M free) Г— $0.20/million          = $1.80
Duration: 0.128GB Г— 0.200s Г— 10M Г— $0.0000166667  = $4.27
                                              TOTAL  = $6.07/month

Same workload on t3.micro EC2 (24/7): $7.49/month
вҶ’ Lambda is cheaper for event-driven, intermittent workloads
вҶ’ Lambda gets EXPENSIVE when called constantly 24/7 вҶ’ use EC2 then
```

### Pricing Models for EC2
```
ON-DEMAND      вҶ’ pay per second, no commitment, highest price
                 Best for: testing, unpredictable workloads, new projects

RESERVED (1-3yr) вҶ’ up to 72% discount, commit to usage
                   Best for: steady 24/7 production servers you'll run 1+ years
                   Types: Standard (specific instance), Convertible (can change)

SAVINGS PLANS  вҶ’ commit to $/hour spend, applies automatically
                 More flexible than Reserved. AWS recommends over Reserved.
                 Best for: production workloads, want flexibility across types

SPOT           вҶ’ up to 90% discount, use AWS spare capacity
                 CATCH: AWS can terminate with 2-minute warning
                 Best for: batch jobs, ML training, fault-tolerant processing
                 NEVER for: databases, real-time APIs, anything stateful

DEDICATED HOST вҶ’ physical server dedicated to you
                 Most expensive. For: per-socket software licenses (Oracle)

FREE TIER      вҶ’ new accounts get 12 months:
                 750 hrs t2.micro/month, 5GB S3, 750hrs RDS t2.micro
                 FOREVER free: 1M Lambda requests/month, 25GB DynamoDB
```

---

> **Core rule:** AWS secures the cloud (the building). You secure IN the cloud (your apartment). Most breaches happen on YOUR side.

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                    CUSTOMER RESPONSIBILITY                      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ              "Security IN the Cloud"                            в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Your Data   в”Ӯ в”Ӯ IAM Policiesв”Ӯ в”Ӯ App Code    в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (encrypt it)в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (least priv)в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (secure it) в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ OS Patches  в”Ӯ в”ӮSecurity Grpsв”Ӯ в”Ӯ Network Cfg в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (EC2 only!) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (firewalls) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (VPC/subnet)в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ

в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ                    AWS RESPONSIBILITY                           в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ              "Security OF the Cloud"                            в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Physical DCs  в”Ӯ  Hardware  в”Ӯ  Hypervisor  в”Ӯ  Global Network   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (guards,      в”Ӯ  (servers, в”Ӯ  (isolation) в”Ӯ  (fiber, routing) в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   cameras)     в”Ӯ   disks)   в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ                   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Responsibility Changes by Service Type

```
EC2 (IaaS) вҖ” YOU handle the most:
  YOUR job:  OS patches, runtime, app code, security groups, data encryption
  AWS job:   Physical hardware, hypervisor

RDS (PaaS) вҖ” AWS handles more:
  YOUR job:  DB schema, user permissions, what data you store, backup settings
  AWS job:   OS patches, DB engine patches, hardware failure, Multi-AZ failover

Lambda (Serverless) вҖ” AWS handles almost everything:
  YOUR job:  Function code, IAM execution role, data your function processes
  AWS job:   OS, runtime patches, scaling, HA, hardware, everything below code

RULE: More managed the service вҶ’ more AWS takes вҶ’ less YOUR security work
```

### Where Breaches Actually Happen
```
IAM access key leaked to GitHub          вҶ’ YOUR fault (99% of cases)
S3 bucket made public with sensitive data вҶ’ YOUR misconfiguration
Security Group: port 3306 open to 0.0.0.0/0 вҶ’ YOUR rule
EC2 running unpatched OS for 6 months    вҶ’ YOUR responsibility
Database password hardcoded in code      вҶ’ YOUR code

AWS infrastructure breached: essentially NEVER at customer-visible level
```

---

в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
### Responsibility Changes by Service Type

EC2 (IaaS) вҖ” YOU handle the most:
  YOUR job:  OS patches, runtime, app code, security groups, data encryption
  AWS job:   Physical hardware, hypervisor

RDS (PaaS) вҖ” AWS handles more:
  YOUR job:  DB schema, user permissions, what data you store, backup settings
  AWS job:   OS patches, DB engine patches, hardware failure, Multi-AZ failover

Lambda (Serverless) вҖ” AWS handles almost everything:
  YOUR job:  Function code, IAM execution role, data your function processes
  AWS job:   OS, runtime patches, scaling, HA, hardware, everything below code

RULE: More managed the service вҶ’ more AWS takes вҶ’ less YOUR security work
### Where Breaches Actually Happen

IAM access key leaked to GitHub          вҶ’ YOUR fault (99% of cases)
S3 bucket made public with sensitive data вҶ’ YOUR misconfiguration
Security Group: port 3306 open to 0.0.0.0/0 вҶ’ YOUR rule
EC2 running unpatched OS for 6 months    вҶ’ YOUR responsibility
Database password hardcoded in code      вҶ’ YOUR code

AWS infrastructure breached: essentially NEVER at customer-visible level

revisionPoints: > **Core rule:** AWS secures the cloud (the building). You secure IN the cloud (your apartment). Most breaches happen on YOUR side.

sections.title: рҹҺҜ Interview Q&A вҖ” Cloud Computing
sections.rawMarkdown:
> рҹ’¬ **Practice tip:** Say each answer OUT LOUD. Target 60вҖ“90 seconds per answer.

**Q: Explain cloud computing to a non-technical executive.**
```
"Cloud computing means renting computing power instead of buying it.
Instead of spending $500,000 upfront on servers, we pay AWS a monthly bill
and scale up or down based on actual demand. Three business benefits:
First, we only pay for what we use вҖ” no wasted idle capacity.
Second, we can launch in a new country in 30 minutes instead of 6 months.
Third, our engineers focus on building our product, not managing hardware.
Netflix, Airbnb, and Slack built billion-dollar companies on AWS without
owning a single server."
```

**Q: What is the Shared Responsibility Model? Where do most breaches happen?**
```
"AWS secures the infrastructure вҖ” physical data centers, hardware, hypervisor,
and managed services. Customers secure their configuration вҖ” IAM policies,
security groups, encryption choices, OS patches on EC2, application code.

Where breaches actually happen: 99% on the customer side.
Most common: leaked IAM access keys, public S3 buckets with sensitive data,
security groups with open ports to the internet, unpatched EC2 instances,
database passwords hardcoded in GitHub.
AWS's physical infrastructure has essentially never been breached at the
customer-visible level. The cloud is secure. Misconfigured clouds are not."
```

**Q: Why would a company NOT move to cloud?**
```
"There are legitimate reasons to stay on-premise or go hybrid:
1. Strict data sovereignty laws вҖ” some countries require data stays physically
   within borders. Even though AWS has local regions, some regulations need
   on-premise physical control.
2. Cost at extreme scale вҖ” running 10,000 servers at 95% utilization 24/7
   for 5+ years: owned bare-metal hardware CAN be cheaper. This is why
   Facebook and Google built their own data centers.
3. Sub-millisecond latency вҖ” high-frequency trading needs < 1ms that cloud
   virtualization overhead can't guarantee. On-premise or colocation wins.
4. Existing hardware investment вҖ” spent $50M on hardware last year.
   Hard to justify abandoning immediately.

For most companies building new applications today: cloud is the right choice.
For large enterprises with stable, fully-utilized workloads: hybrid makes sense."
```

**Q: Is multi-tenancy safe? My code runs on the same hardware as other companies?**
```
"Yes, demonstrably safe for the vast majority of workloads. AWS uses hardware-
level isolation via the Nitro hypervisor вҖ” each VM gets dedicated virtual CPU,
RAM, and storage. Memory isolation is hardware-enforced, not just software.
Even if a neighboring VM crashes or is compromised, yours is unaffected.

For specific regulated workloads with strict compliance needs вҖ” classified
government, some financial systems вҖ” EC2 Dedicated Hosts provide physical
hardware dedicated solely to your account, eliminating the multi-tenancy
concern while keeping all other cloud benefits."
```

**Q: What are the 5 characteristics of cloud and can you give an example that violates each?**
```
"1. On-demand self-service вҖ” violated by: managed hosting where you email
   support to add capacity. On-demand yes, self-service no.

2. Broad network access вҖ” violated by: a mainframe accessible only via
   proprietary terminal software on a company VPN. Not broad.

3. Resource pooling вҖ” violated by: a Dedicated Host. Still cloud but
   breaks multi-tenancy. Technically a gray area.

4. Rapid elasticity вҖ” violated by: shared hosting where you fill a form
   and wait 24 hours to upgrade. Not rapid.

5. Measured service вҖ” violated by: flat-rate unlimited hosting.
   Not measured, can't optimize cost per actual usage."
```

---
